concerns.text: The plan proposes fitting a linear regression to performance vs. log(context_length) using only 5 data points (32K, 64K, 128K, 256K, 512K). With n=5, the statistical power to distinguish between linear, sublinear, and superlinear trends is negligible, and the slope coefficient will be extremely sensitive to noise. This sample size is insufficient to validate the scaling law claim (Geoffrey West's concern) or support any causal inference about the relationship between context length and performance.

The plan states: 'If the implementation cannot support [Benjamini-Hochberg] due to resource constraints, the report will explicitly state this as a limitation.' This is a methodological failure. The claim of 'generalization beyond 128K' relies on comparing performance across multiple context lengths and task categories. Without multiple-comparison correction, the probability of a false positive (Type I error) is unacceptably high. A limitation statement does not validate the claim; the analysis must be designed to control FDR or the claim must be retracted.

The 'Generalization Claim Validation' defines retention as Score_256K / Score_128K. This is a correlational metric. The plan does not account for the fact that the model was likely trained on data up to 128K. A drop in performance at 256K is expected due to distribution shift, not necessarily a failure of the architecture. The methodology lacks a control for the 'extrapolation penalty' or a baseline comparison (e.g., a model trained only on short contexts) to isolate the effect of the long-context training.

The plan relies on a sample size of 10 per context length (n=10). For a 7B parameter model, the variance in performance on a single sample can be high due to stochasticity in generation or specific document difficulty. With n=10, the standard error of the mean will be large, making it impossible to detect small but meaningful differences (e.g., the ±1.0% tolerance mentioned in SC-002) with statistical significance. The power analysis is missing.
